Great! Even when only asking them if they have a few minutes to talk. You want to steer clear of yeses as much as possible. Yes, nearly always feels like some sort of a trap. Some people see yes as a positive word that here people tend to interpret any form of hearing it as a victory. And since we believe that no is the opposite of yes, when we hear no, we assume that it's a failure. I need you to stop thinking this way immediately. It's important for me to describe to you why no is so valuable. Let's take a deeper critical look at the word no. First, hear all the things that no isn't. It isn't an abuse of power. It isn't an act of rejection. It isn't a manifestation of stubbornness. And it isn't the end of the negotiation. The word no is a reaffirmation of autonomy. And it protects and benefits all parties in the exchange by creating feelings of safety, security, and feelings of control. Getting them to say that magic two-letter word is a requirement if you're ever going to achieve success in the negotiation world. Ask your counterpart a question that prompts a no. And your counterpart feels like they are in the driver's seat and dictating the conversation. Great negotiators welcome and even invite a solid no. It's a clear indicator that the other side is engaged. Giving your counterpart the feeling of control is a great way to open the path forward and achieve your desired outcomes. If you master no, then you cease to be the hostage of yes because you're no longer desperate to hear yes. Getting your counterpart to say no is ridiculously easy. You'll be stunned and I mean stunned at what people will comfortably say no to. For example, we've coached people to ask their boss, do you want me to fail when given an extremely difficult or impossible task? We imagine that the boss will scream at us, of course not, and maybe fire us. But in reality, they answer in a very supportive way and give people even more guidance because they feel safe by saying no. Please note, only use this with tasks that are actually extremely difficult or nearly impossible. Another example is if you're talking to someone and they are obviously not listening. The best way to get their attention back is by provoking them into a no. A really simple yet effective technique that I found works wonders is by using a label with a twist. This time, you want to mislabel their emotion or desire. It forces them to listen and makes them feel comfortable correcting you. Another great way to extract a no from someone is to straight up ask the other party what they don't want. People are comfortable saying no here because they feel like they're protecting themselves. The sooner that someone says no, the sooner they're willing and able to see options they were blind to previously. Saying no often gives people the prompt they need to move forward. Since they established a comfortable level of protection, they want to seize the opportunity before it slips away. Having just said no, people very often begin to think through the how and begin to describe implementation. This is what we call a calibrated no. And a calibrated no is usually worth five yeses, five. Imagine how much time that can save you. Here's a couple of quick examples. Instead of saying does this look like a solution that would work for you, say is it ridiculous to think this would work for you? Instead of saying would you like me to do this? Say are you against having me do this? Instead of saying do you agree? Say do you disagree? No is actually a great word that is a lot of different applications and results. The word no allows the real issues to be brought forth, protects people from making ineffective decisions and lets them correct those ineffective decisions, slows things down so that people can freely embrace their decisions and the agreements they enter into. Helps people feel safe, secure, mostly comfortable and in control of their decision and moves everyone's efforts forward. Finally, when you finally get your counterpart to utter that beautiful two letter word, you should feel liberated. The negotiation can then move forward.
